hub_subtitle: Inglês para Viagens & Cotidiano com Foco em Listening

lesson.menu_desc: Report the travel advice locals give you as you learn how different American cities move -- reported speech -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- Getting Around Like a Local
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 16 -- Getting Around Like a Local

listenings.text: Hi Lucimara, it is Sofia. So you asked me how to get around when you visit next month, and here is my honest advice. Downtown is completely walkable, so you will not need a car for most of the trip. The streets follow a grid system, so they are numbered and really easy to find. One warning: if you take a rideshare during rush hour, be careful, because the app uses surge pricing and you will pay a lot more. My real tip is to walk or take the subway crosstown, and if a street is blocked, the app will reroute you around the bottleneck. Oh, and there is a beautiful bike lane along the river if you want to rent a bike for a day. Call me when you land. Bye!

Here is a quick tip for reporting what other people say to you. When you tell a friend what someone said, you usually move the verb one step into the past. If your friend says I live here, you report it as she said that she lived here. Present becomes past. Will becomes would, so I will help you becomes he said that he would help me. And can becomes could, so I can drive becomes she said that she could drive. Two verbs to remember: use say with no person, she said that, but use tell with a person, she told me that. Get those right, and you can report any piece of advice you hear on your trip.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
easy to get around on foot
b
streets laid out in a regular pattern of numbered blocks
c
the spread of a city outward over a large area
d
to share a car ride with other people going the same way
e
higher rideshare prices when a lot of people want a ride
f
a marked part of the road only for bicycles
g
to change a planned route to avoid a problem
h
a narrow point where traffic slows down and backs up
i
going from one side of the city to the other
j
walking instead of using a car or transport
k
a place on a road or bridge where you stop to pay
l
to signal a taxi or bus to stop for you

reading.rtitle: The City Decides How You Move
paras: Before her first long trip across the United States, Lucimara asked three friends for advice, and each one told her something different. A colleague who lived in New York said that she would barely need a car, because the city was walkable and the subway ran everywhere. A friend in Los Angeles laughed and told her that she would be lost without one, since the city was built around the highway and the urban sprawl went on for miles.

In New York, everything follows a grid system, so her friend explained that the streets were numbered and easy to remember. He said that she should walk or take the subway, and that a rideshare in crosstown traffic would only trap her in a bottleneck. In Los Angeles, the story was the opposite. Her friend there told her that most people drove or used a carpool, that rideshare apps raised prices with surge pricing during rush hour, and that she would spend more time on the road than she expected.

By the end of the week, Lucimara understood the real lesson. There was no single American way to get around. In some cities you move on foot; in others you never leave your car. The smartest travelers, one friend had told her, always ask a local first, because the person who lives there knows which route saves you time and which one costs you an hour of your day.
reading.source: Adapted from a United States city travel guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: New York is the easiest American city to get around.
There is no single way to get around in the US; it depends on the city, so the smartest travelers ask a local first.
You always need your own car to travel in the United States.

items: A friend told Lucimara that she would need a car in New York.
The New York friend said that she would barely need a car, because the city was walkable and the subway ran everywhere.
In New York, the streets follow a grid system and are numbered.
Her friend explained that the streets were numbered and easy to remember.
In Los Angeles, most people get around on foot.
Her friend there told her that most people drove or used a carpool.
The text says the smartest travelers ask a local how to get around.
One friend had told her that the smartest travelers always ask a local first.

gapfill.text: Reported speech: use SAY with no person (she said that the city was walkable) and TELL with a person (he told me that I would need a car). Move the verb one step back: present becomes past (live becomes lived), will becomes would, and can becomes could.

bank.label: Useful language for reporting advice
items: He told me that the city was walkable.
She said that I would need a car.
My friend explained that the streets were numbered.
They told me that rideshare prices went up at rush hour.
A local said that the subway was the fastest way.
She told me that I could walk everywhere downtown.

lf.title: Complete the reported sentence
items: Direct: I live in New York. Reported: She said that she
Direct: You will love the ferry. Reported: He told me that I
Direct: The streets are numbered. Reported: She said that the streets
Direct: You can walk everywhere. Reported: He told me that I

lf.text: Back-shift the verb by one step: present becomes past (live becomes lived, are becomes were), will becomes would, can becomes could. Use SAY with no person and TELL with a person.

items.situation: Your friend said: 'The city is walkable.' Report it to someone else.
tips: Use 'said that' and move the verb back.
'She said that the city was walkable.'

items.situation: A local said: 'You will need a car.' Report it to a friend.
tips: 'He told me that I would need a car.'

items.situation: The app said: 'Prices are higher now.' Report it.
tips: 'The app said that prices were higher then.'

items.situation: Your colleague said: 'I can walk to work.' Report it.
tips: 'She said that she could walk to work.'
Keep the meaning, change the tense.

items.situation: A friend said: 'The subway runs all night.' Report it.
tips: 'He said that the subway ran all night.'

items: Report the advice your New York friend gave you about getting around. Use 'She told me that...' and back-shift the verb.
Report what a local told you about rideshare prices at rush hour. Use 'He said that...'
Tell a friend what the app said about the fastest route. Change the present verb to the past.
Report three pieces of travel advice you received, using 'said' and 'told' correctly at least once each.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = told, 2 = walkable, 3 = said, 4 = sprawl, 5 = explained.
SAY takes no person: 'She said (that) the city was walkable.' TELL takes a person: 'He told me (that) I would need a car.'
Back-shift: present becomes past (live becomes lived, are becomes were); will becomes would; can becomes could.
Time and place words shift too: here becomes there, now becomes then, today becomes that day.
answerkey.note: SAY = no person (say that...). TELL = a person (tell me that...). Move the verb one step into the past.
